findings.title: Saturation-based stopping for iterative extraction: quality plateaus by iteration ~3; stop when new-item yield drops
findings.claim: Measured evidence exists for 'keep extracting until marginal value < threshold': in LLM entity extraction, re-running the extractor to find items 'not extracted yet' improves completeness, but from the third iteration (4 total calls) on a ~12k-char document 'the majority of scores are either getting worse or stagnating' (semantic similarity peaks at iter 3 = 0.7572 then drops; bias-avoidance degrades 0.5614->0.4247). The paper's stated principle: 'stop when additional LLM call will return no or only a few new entities', with iteration count scaling with document length. Separately, an adaptive early-stopping rule 'stop when current marginal gain falls below a fraction omega of the first-step gain' appears in 2025-26 agentic-loop work. This is the strongest verified pattern for a content-driven item count: the video's ideal clip count = size of the inventory at saturation, not a constant.

sources.note: Fetched full text. Table 2 iteration study: quotes and numbers above verified; recommends 3 iterations for ~12k chars and stopping when few new entities are returned; explicitly notes shorter/longer texts need fewer/more iterations.
Search-level only (not fetched): agentic loop-optimization work described as using adaptive early stopping when marginal gain < omega * first-step gain; treat the omega rule as a lead until fetched.

findings.adoption: Replace MAX_ANCHORS (config.py:210) as the binding count with a saturation-derived budget: add an 'inventory pass' before selection that repeatedly asks the extractor for 'important teachable items not yet listed' (grounded in structure.json units) and stops when a pass yields < ~15% new items vs the first pass (2-3 passes for a 15-min lecture per the 12k-char guidance). budget = clamp(len(inventory), 8, 32); pass it into candidates.py select_anchors and assemble max_clips (config.py:112 MAX_SEGMENTS becomes a safety rail, not a target). Eval: log inventory size and saturation curve per video in run_eval; for qP-9wwRrJbg the inventory should land ~18-22, directly testing gap #1/#2.

findings.title: Variable-cardinality moment retrieval (GMR) is now an explicit task with set-completeness metrics — the field is abandoning fixed-K
findings.claim: Generalized (Video) Moment Retrieval, formalized at ICLR 2025 and extended by Soccer-GMR (May 2026), requires returning the COMPLETE SET of relevant moments per query — zero, one, or many — instead of a fixed top-K. Soccer-GMR ships an evaluation protocol with 'complementary metrics tailored for null-set rejection, positive-query localization, and end-to-end GMR performance', and two modeling paradigms (a lightweight adapter for discriminative models; GRPO reward fine-tuning for MLLMs). This is direct precedent for (a) threshold/set-based selection rather than top-K caps, and (b) eval metrics that penalize both missed items and hallucinated ones.

sources.note: Fetched abstract. Verifies GMR task definition ('retrieving the complete set of relevant moments or predicting an empty set'), Soccer-GMR benchmark, null-set-rejection metrics, two baselines; code+dataset on GitHub.
ICLR 2025 'Generalized Video Moment Retrieval' — non-target and multi-target queries, BCANet baseline (search-level; not fetched).

findings.adoption: candidates.py: convert the flat priority sort (candidates.py:106) from take-top-12 to take-all-above-threshold (score >= tau) with the cap as clamp upper bound 32; tune tau on the golden set so predicted-set size tracks golden-inventory size. Eval: replace 'N clips produced' with set metrics — inventory recall (fraction of golden items covered by any shipped clip), precision (clips matching no golden item), and a null-behavior check for genuinely thin videos. This makes gap #2 (no clip-count policy) measurable.

findings.title: Continuous LLM saliency scoring with sliding-window batching: cross-genre, no fixed count, 6x cost cut from batching (HiVid, Feb 2026)
findings.claim: HiVid scores video-chunk saliency with an LLM as 'a scalable human proxy': frames grouped into windows of m=10 per call with a periodically-updated running summary as context, plus an LLM-guided merge-sort ranking for global score consistency. It produces continuous [0,1] weights with NO predetermined highlight count. Cross-category generalization is measured: PLCC 0.66 / 0.50 / 0.47 on YouTube-8M / TVSum / SumMe (+11.5% over next best), mAP@50 0.86, stable even on sensitive content, and works with open-source models (Llama, Qwen, InternVL) at modest gaps. Cost: batching 10 items/call cuts per-video cost from $8.12 to $1.35 (201s video, GPT-4o) — direct evidence that windowed batching keeps LLM scoring cost sublinear in item count.

sources.note: Fetched full HTML. Verifies sliding-window m=10, periodic summary context, merge-sort ranking, PLCC/mAP/user-study numbers, $8.12->$1.35 batching cost comparison, open-model compatibility, no fixed highlight count.

findings.adoption: Use as the cheap pre-filter for gap #8: one batched pass scores all 139 units (10 units/call with a running video summary = ~14 cheap calls) on a saliency rubric; only units above calibrated tau proceed to the full judge in validate.py. Also adopt merge-sort pairwise ranking to break the 27-way 0.70 priority ties in candidates.py:106 instead of list-order (front-of-video) tie-breaking. Eval: PLCC-style correlation of unit saliency vs golden-inventory membership.

findings.title: Batch prompting makes judge cost sublinear: 30-82% cost cuts with ~3pp accuracy loss, sometimes accuracy GAINS; reasoning-token collapse 4.5x
findings.claim: Packing multiple independent items into one judge call is well-measured: across 7 LLMs and 6 benchmarks, batch evaluation loses only ~3.2pp absolute vs single-item (75.5%->72.3%) while cutting inference cost 30.7-82.0%; accuracy is stable for batch sizes below ~8-16 depending on task. A mid-2025 title/abstract-screening study found batch size 15 IMPROVED accuracy 75.0%->78.2% vs single-query. For reasoning models, batching suppresses overthinking (reasoning tokens 2950->710, 4.5x reduction). RoBatch (May 2026) jointly optimizes model routing + batch size along a cost-utility Pareto frontier with a batch-aware degradation proxy (Qwen3/Gemma3, six benchmarks).

sources.note: 'Evaluating LLMs with Multiple Problems at once' — source of the 3.2pp / 30.7-82% numbers (search-summary level; numbers consistent across two search hits but not independently fetched).
Batch-size-15 accuracy gain 75.0->78.2% in screening with mid-2025 SOTA LLMs (search-summary level).
Batch prompting suppresses overthinking; 2950->710 reasoning tokens (search-summary level).
Fetched abstract. RoBatch: Route-with-Batching problem, batch-aware proxy utility model + greedy Pareto scheduling; superior cost-performance frontier vs routing-only and batching-only baselines.

findings.adoption: validate.py: judge 5-8 candidate specs per call (shared system prompt + rubric amortized) instead of 1 judge/candidate x JUDGE_WORKERS=4. At cap ~24, first-pass judging becomes 3-5 calls (vs 24), fitting gemini-2.5-flash-lite free-tier limits; run JUDGE_MAX_REPAIR re-judges and kill-confirmations individually (only failures). Add an A/B eval: batched vs single-item verdict agreement rate before switching.

findings.title: Cheap judges recover accuracy via criteria injection + small-model ensembles, not escalation (April 2026)
findings.claim: A systematic study of cost-effective LLM-judge improvements (OpenAI + Anthropic families) finds ensemble scoring + task-specific criteria injection dominates the cost-accuracy Pareto frontier — up to 85.8% accuracy, +13.5pp over baseline — with criteria injection 'virtually cost free' and small models benefiting disproportionately from ensembling. Calibration context and adaptive model escalation improve baselines but are NOT cost-competitive. Implication: scaling candidate count 12->30 does not require a bigger judge; it requires per-task criteria in the prompt and cheap self-consistency on borderline cases.

sources.note: Fetched abstract. Verifies the four techniques, 85.8% / +13.5pp, criteria-injection-plus-ensemble Pareto dominance, cross-provider generalization, and that escalation is not cost-competitive.

findings.adoption: validate.py gate: inject the per-clip contract-by-content criteria (Wave 2 already produces them) directly into the judge prompt as explicit rubric lines (free accuracy). For borderline verdicts only (e.g., within epsilon of the pass threshold), take a 3-vote ensemble of the same flash-lite judge at temperature>0 rather than escalating models. Eval: track judge-verdict flip rate on the frozen-spec harness (the existing --freeze/--runs variance machinery) to confirm noise reduction.

findings.title: Coverage-gap-driven iterative generation (CCQGen pattern): condition each new round on concepts NOT yet covered
findings.claim: CCQGen (WSDM 2025) generates a query set for a document by explicitly identifying 'concepts not sufficiently covered by previous queries' and conditioning subsequent generation on those gaps so each new item complements the existing set; experiments show significant gains in coverage/retrieval quality vs non-adaptive generation. This is the verified algorithmic template for both the missing dedupe-refund loop (gap #7) and exhaustive enumeration: generation/selection loops should be driven by the residual uncovered-concept set, not by a fixed quota computed once.

sources.note: Fetched abstract. Verifies the iterative uncovered-concept conditioning mechanism and claimed significant improvements (specific numbers require full PDF).

findings.adoption: assemble/__init__.py after snap_candidates/dedupe: compute residual = anchor-eligible units (and content-map nodes) not overlapped by any surviving spec; if residual non-empty and len(specs) < budget, re-run candidates.py selection restricted to residual units and assemble another round (repeat until residual empty or budget hit) — in the qP-9wwRrJbg reconstruction this refund loop alone recovers ~10-12 specs from the 63 untouched eligible units. Eval metric: node coverage = content-map nodes with >=1 shipped clip / total nodes (this video: target 12/12, actual 2/15-ish).

findings.title: Omission auditing with statistical guarantees: conformal risk control bounds the fraction of important content missed (CARE, June 2026)
findings.claim: CARE adds a post-hoc, model-agnostic 'omission controller' to summarization: it scores source sentences for importance and coverage, and uses conformal risk control over the joint (tau, gamma) threshold space to guarantee — finite-sample, distribution-free — that the expected fraction of important omissions NOT surfaced for review is bounded (target alpha=0.15 achieved with 95% confidence across 100 calibration/test resplits, using only ~100 labeled documents per domain). A 75-review clinician study showed calibrated flags improved human omission detection by +28.6pp. This is the only verified 2025-26 mechanism that answers 'how do you verify nothing was missed' with a quantitative guarantee, and it needs exactly the scale of golden set this project already plans.

sources.note: Fetched abstract. Verifies dual hallucination/omission controllers, joint threshold calibration, alpha=0.15 @95% guarantee, ~100 labeled docs/domain, +28.6pp clinician result, five medical summarization tasks; design is model-agnostic (transfer beyond medical untested).

findings.adoption: Eval harness (run_eval): add an omission-audit stage — cheap importance score for every unit (reuse the HiVid-style batched scorer), then flag units above calibrated tau that no shipped clip covers; calibrate tau on the planned golden set (~100 labeled units suffices per CARE) and report 'bounded-miss rate' per run alongside the judge pass rate. This turns the user's complaint ('there has to be a lot of other stuff in there') into a monitored, guaranteed metric rather than an anecdote.

findings.title: 2024-26 educational QG/quiz pipelines use fixed per-lecture counts + deterministic pre-judge QC; none measure exhaustiveness — but two components transfer
findings.claim: Surveyed lecture-to-items systems do NOT solve coverage-completeness: L2Q (2026) generates a fixed 8 MCQs/lecture with a local LLM but achieves ~98% acceptance via deterministic QC (JSON schema conformance, single-correct-answer validation, numeric/constant equivalence testing, warning layer) under bounded retries — cheap rule checks BEFORE any LLM judging. COSER (2025, ~500 educator-annotated questions over 35 lectures) shows LLM-selected answer-relevant context beats using the full transcript for generation quality (best NLI 41.09 with transcript+keyframes+rewriting), i.e., focused per-item context wins. Neither measures whether all teachable content was covered — exhaustive extraction is an open niche, and coverage metrics must come from elsewhere (CCQGen/CARE patterns).

sources.note: Fetched abstract. Verifies self-hosted pipeline, deterministic QC checks listed, 120/122 accepted (~98%), 8 questions/lecture fixed, no coverage quantification.
Fetched. Verifies COSER task, AIRC dataset (27+8 lectures, ~500 questions), CoT context selection > fixed windows, full-transcript-only suboptimal, coverage only implicit.

findings.adoption: Two adoptions: (1) pre-judge deterministic QC in assemble — before any validate.py call, run rule checks per candidate (duration bounds, sentence-boundary alignment, contract fields present, quote-in-transcript exact match) and reject/repair without LLM cost, cutting judge load as the cap rises; (2) COSER's context finding supports Wave 2's contract-by-content: build each clip's judge context from its own span + linked prerequisite units, not the whole transcript.

findings.title: Commercial study tools (NotebookLM, Turbo AI) expose item count as a USER control with grounding — no tool claims exhaustiveness
findings.claim: Google NotebookLM (flashcards/quizzes GA since Sept 2025 for Education/Workspace) lets users 'choose how many cards to make', set difficulty, and customize topic, with generation 'grounded only in your source materials'; Turbo AI (formerly TurboLearn) converts video/audio/PDF into notes+flashcards+quizzes at scale. No commercial tool documents an exhaustiveness guarantee or an automatic content-driven count — the market pattern is user-adjustable count + source grounding. This validates a product split the pipeline currently lacks (gap #9): 'give me everything important' (auto/adaptive count) vs 'search within video' (query-filtered) as distinct modes, with count as an exposed dial.

sources.note: Google's announcement: flashcards/quizzes generated from sources, customizable count/difficulty/topic (search-level; consistent with support page).
Official help doc for generating flashcards/quizzes with user-set counts.
Workspace GA rollout for education, Sept 2025.
Turbo AI marketing page: video/audio/PDF -> notes, flashcards, quizzes (no coverage guarantee documented).

findings.adoption: config/API surface: make max_clips a tri-state — 'auto' (saturation-derived budget, default), explicit integer (user dial, NotebookLM-style), or query mode. Fix topic-filter semantics: when the query fuzzy-matches detection.rationale/title (as 'kinematics' does here), switch to 'auto/everything' mode and short-circuit score_topic_relevance to 1.0 (the existing empty-topic path at candidates.py:62-63), eliminating the class-(c) tangential-item kills.

findings.title: A single genre-agnostic LLM rubric generalizes across genres without genre adapters (Prompts-to-Summaries; corroborated by HiVid)
findings.claim: Prompts-to-Summaries (2025-26, v3) is fully training-free: scenes are described by a VideoLM, then an LLM scores each scene 1-100 against the WHOLE-VIDEO context using a five-band rubric; scores propagate to frames via consistency+uniqueness weighting. Results: SumMe F1 56.84 (beats all unsupervised methods), TVSum 62.22, competitive with supervised approaches — across the heterogeneous genres of both benchmarks with one universal rubric. HiVid independently shows stable LLM saliency across YouTube-8M's category spread. This is the best available evidence that open-vocabulary, prompt-driven importance works across arbitrary genres; note neither work compares against per-genre adapter baselines directly, so 'LLM-proposed per-video schema beats fixed ontology' remains unproven in the video domain — the verified claim is that universal prompting reaches SOTA-unsupervised without any genre specialization.

sources.note: Fetched. Verifies zero-shot claim, five-band 1-100 rubric with full-video context, consistency/uniqueness propagation, SumMe 56.84 / TVSum 62.22, no training data.
Fetched. HiVid cross-category PLCC stability (0.66/0.50/0.47) including sensitive categories — corroborates genre-agnostic LLM importance.
Context-aware pseudo-label LLM scene scoring for zero-shot summarization (search-level; supporting).

findings.adoption: candidates.py: add a universal 'teachable-moment' rubric score (1-100, five bands, scored against a video-level summary for context) computed in the same batched pre-filter pass, and blend it with role priority (e.g., 0.5*priority + 0.5*rubric) so items the fixed taxonomy mislabels (exam tips labeled 'claim', misconception non-anchors — gap #4) can still win slots. Eval: A/B adapter-priority-only vs blended on golden inventory recall.

findings.title: Adaptive/retrieved schemas outperform rigid fixed schemas in structured extraction (ASEE + 2025-26 KG-construction consensus)
findings.claim: ASEE (2025) targets 'rigid schema fixation in existing pipeline systems': it paraphrases schemas, retrieves relevant ones from hundreds of candidates (schema-RAG), then generates against the retrieved schema, 'significantly improving the accuracy of event extraction' on MD-SEE, a new 12-dataset multi-domain benchmark built specifically because no schema-adaptivity benchmark existed. The 2025-26 LLM-KG-construction literature converges on the same hybrid: predefined ontologies for precision, dynamically generated/expanded schemas for coverage of out-of-ontology content. Direct quantified head-to-head numbers (LLM-induced vs fixed, same data) were not extractable from abstracts — the mechanism-level conclusion stands; magnitudes need the MD-SEE tables.

sources.note: Fetched abstract. Verifies schema paraphrasing + schema-RAG + targeted generation, MD-SEE (12 datasets), 'significantly improving accuracy' vs rigid pipelines; exact deltas not in abstract.
LLM-empowered KG construction survey: frames schema-based vs open/dynamic-schema paradigms as complementary (search-level).

findings.adoption: Supports Wave 2's per-video extraction plan and extends it: instead of one fixed 22-role taxonomy, maintain a LIBRARY of role schemas (lecture, cooking, golf-lesson, debate...) and let the plan pass retrieve+paraphrase the closest one, permitting plan-proposed novel roles (exam_tip, misconception_correction) that must bind to contract-by-content evidence. This addresses gap #4 without unbounded open-vocabulary noise. Eval: count of shipped clips anchored on plan-proposed (non-taxonomy) roles that pass the judge.

findings.title: Serving evidence: <5-minute clips get measurably higher engagement in real coursework; organization evidence is KG-path-shaped but thin on RCTs
findings.claim: Best post-ReelsEd data found: a 3-year observational cohort (n=152 med students, 173 flipped-classroom neurosurgery videos on Edpuzzle) — videos <5 min had significantly higher audience retention (median 92% vs 91%, p=.039), much higher embedded-question response rates (51% vs 39%, p=.045), and better viewing index after regression (p=.049); authors flag retrospective design and engagement!=learning. A 2025 cross-sectional pharmacology study (n=77) is directionally consistent. For ORGANIZING 20-30 clips, the dominant 2025-26 pattern is knowledge-graph-based learning-path sequencing (survey: Electronics 2026; FKGRec adds forgetting-aware RL paths, Mar 2026), but effectiveness evidence is mostly simulation/offline — no RCT found comparing clip organizations. Net: more/shorter beats fewer/longer on engagement (medium evidence); prerequisite-ordered grouping is the consensus organization (weak outcome evidence).

sources.note: Fetched full text. Verifies n=152, 173 videos, 3 years, six engagement metrics, <5min significance (p=.039/.045/.049) and exact medians, plus stated limitations.
2025 cross-sectional pharmacology short-video study (n=77) — directional support only (search-level).
2026 survey: personalized learning-path recommendation on knowledge graphs — the organization pattern, evidence mostly non-RCT (search-level).
FKGRec: forgetting-factor + KG-aware path recommendation (search-level).

findings.adoption: assemble budget/config: set a soft target clip duration <=300s and make the mash-splitter (gap #6 fix) prefer splitting any multi-idea clip exceeding it (clip 1 at 217s is fine by length but fails one-idea; use both criteria). Serving: order shipped clips prerequisite-first using dependencies.py edges grouped by content-map node (chapter grouping) — which raises the priority of fixing the requires-edge starvation (gap #5, the concepts_introduced prompt nudge). Eval: report %clips<=300s and %clips with resolved prerequisites-before-them in sequence.

leads: InfoMerge / Content-Aware Budget Allocation (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.02161): allocates token budget by segment-level information content (uniqueness + representational richness) instead of segment length — the closest thing found to measured 'density-based scaling'; transfer the estimator to scale clip budget with unit-information density. Not fetched; read Sec. on CABA.

Agentic Auto-Scheduling (https://arxiv.org/html/2511.00592v1): the 'stop when marginal gain < omega * first-step gain' adaptive rule — verify it's this paper and lift the omega formulation for the saturation stopping criterion.

Tuning LLM Judge Design Decisions for 1/1000 of the Cost (https://arxiv.org/abs/2501.17178): multi-objective multi-fidelity search over judge prompt/model/config; relevant if judge config is re-tuned after batching.

PAJAMA program-synthesis judges ($133-184 -> $0.053 per 60K examples, from search summary only): if real, distills judge rubrics into executable checks — extreme version of the deterministic pre-judge QC; find the primary paper.

DecMetrics (https://arxiv.org/abs/2509.04483): COMPLETENESS/CORRECTNESS/SEMANTIC-ENTROPY metrics for claim decomposition used as an RL reward for a lightweight decomposition model — the completeness formula (joint coverage of input) could become the per-video inventory-completeness metric; abstract lacks formulas, read full PDF.
Soccer-GMR code+dataset (GitHub, via https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02623): concrete implementations of null-set-rejection and complete-set metrics to copy into run_eval.

Video-MMLU (https://arxiv.org/abs/2504.14693): lecture-video benchmark (detailed-notes + quiz tasks per search descriptions, 90+ models evaluated) — potential external benchmark for coverage of lecture content; abstract fetch did not expose the metric details, read the project page.

HiERO-StepG (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.31227): zero-shot step grounding, 2nd place Ego4D Step Grounding 2026 — steps/substeps emerge from co-occurrence structure without procedure annotations; candidate span-level corroboration signal for arcs.py to de-noise role-label-dependent arc detection (gap #3).
EQGBench (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2508.10005): educational QG evaluation with a 'knowledge point alignment' metric — possible template for measuring whether clips align to enumerated knowledge points.

DIAL-SUMMER (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.08149): hierarchical error taxonomy for dialogue summaries including omission classes — alternative omission-audit rubric if CARE-style calibration is too heavy.

CVPR 2025 'Video Summarization with Large Language Models' (https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/CVPR2025/papers/Lee_Video_Summarization_with_Large_Language_Models_CVPR_2025_paper.pdf): LLM segment importance scores — another universal-scoring datapoint; check if it handles adaptive summary length.

Context-Aware Pseudo-Label Scoring for Zero-Shot Video Summarization (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2510.17501): reason-mining on top/bottom-scored segments — cheap explanation harvesting could feed judge criteria injection.

LVSum (https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.10024): 13-domain timestamp-aware long-video summarization benchmark with LLM-based content-relevance metrics — fetched abstract only; the human summaries' segment counts per video could empirically calibrate 'ideal clip count vs video length/density'.

No published follow-up to ReelsEd (n=62) with a proper RCT on many-short-clips vs few-long-clips learning OUTCOMES was found in 2025-26 searches — the Edpuzzle cohort (engagement, observational) is the current best; treat outcome claims as open.
notes: Method: ~10 web searches + 14 source fetches (July 2026 vantage; arXiv IDs 26xx = 2026 papers). Confidence policy: 'high' = full text or abstract fetched and numbers verified; 'medium' = abstract-level verification or search-summary numbers consistent across multiple hits; load-bearing but unfetched items were demoted to leads. Direct answers to the four questions: (Q1) No off-the-shelf 'ideal clip count' estimator exists; the verified composite is saturation-stopped enumeration (2404.04068) to SET the budget, threshold/set-based selection instead of top-K (GMR), and continuous batched LLM saliency (HiVid) to rank — all three fetched. Cost side is the best-evidenced area: batching (30-82% cheaper, ~3pp loss or gains) + free criteria injection + deterministic pre-judge QC means 30 candidates/video can cost LESS than today's 12 individual judge calls: e.g., 14 batched scoring calls (10 units each) + 4-5 batched judge calls (6-8 specs each) + individual re-judges only on failures. (Q2) No academic or commercial system verified claims exhaustive coverage; the transferable pieces are CCQGen's uncovered-concept-conditioned iteration (= the missing dedupe-refund loop, gap #7) and CARE's conformally-calibrated omission bound (needs only ~100 labeled units — matches the planned golden set). Exhaustive extraction is a genuine open niche — a defensible product edge. (Q3) Universal prompt-rubric importance is SOTA-unsupervised across genres (Prompts-to-Summaries, HiVid) but nobody has published LLM-per-video-schema vs fixed-ontology head-to-head on VIDEO; text-side (ASEE) favors adaptive/retrieved schemas mechanically. Recommendation: schema-library retrieval + plan-proposed novel roles bound to evidence, A/B'd on golden inventory recall — do not fully abandon the adapter prior. (Q4) Engagement data favors <=5-min clips (p<.05 on three metrics, observational n=152); organization consensus is prerequisite-first KG paths but outcome RCTs are absent — so dependency-ordered chapter grouping is justified as UX, not as proven pedagogy. Gap-list mapping: findings 1-2 -> gaps #1/#2; 3-5 -> #8; 6 -> #7; 7 -> eval/verification; 8 -> pre-judge QC + contract context; 9 -> #9; 10 -> #3/#4 (partial, via rubric blend); 11 -> #4; 12 -> #5/#6. Quick-win validation from evidence: quick-wins (1) cap scaling and (5) refund loop are directly supported (findings 1, 2, 6); (2) relevance bypass is supported by the NotebookLM mode-split pattern (finding 9); (3) tie-breaking by spread is strengthened by HiVid's merge-sort global ranking (finding 3); (7) priority-floor revisit becomes necessary once selection goes threshold-based (finding 2). Caveats: Soccer-GMR is sports-domain; CARE is medical (mechanism model-agnostic, transfer unproven); batch-prompting degradation is task-dependent — run the agreement A/B before enabling; Edpuzzle cohort measures engagement, not learning outcomes. No repo files were modified; no live LLM calls made."
